fund.name: ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund - Direct Plan (Growth)

allocations.rationale: Half your monthly money here for stability. Since you are in the New Tax Regime, you can't get 80C benefits anyway, so we choose this safe fund over risky options. It behaves like a Fixed Deposit with slightly better long-term potential.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Credit Risk Fund - Direct Plan (Bonus)

allocations.rationale: This put your money in slightly longer-term government bonds and high-quality corporate bonds. It's safer than pure stocks but gives you a bit more growth than overnight funds. Think of it as a steady oxidation meant to protect you from keeping too much cash when you retire.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan (Growth)

allocations.rationale: You're 58, so we keep stocks low to protect your wealth. But since you want ₹8 Cr/month later, we can't go 100% safe. This mixes large and mid-sized companies for steady, long-term growth without the wild swings of small stocks.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on apps like Groww, Kuvera, Coin, or the MFU app.
Step 2: Start SIPs for the above three funds. Set up auto-debit for ₹13,000 (Overnight), ₹7,800 (Credit Risk), and ₹5,200 (Large & Mid) from your bank account every month.
Step 3: Since you have ₹70.2 lakhs in existing FDs maturing, don't keep them all in Fixed Deposits. Start transferring the maturing shares into your mutual fund portfolio. Split them as: 50% into the Overnight Fund, 30% into the Credit Risk Fund, and 20% into the Large & Mid Cap Fund.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if your SIPs are running correctly and if the funds have been invested.
After 1 year: Compare the returns of these funds with their main rivals. If a fund is performing much worse than similar funds, consult an advisor before switching.

At the end of Year 3: Rebalance. Since you're only 62 then, ensure your stock portion (Large & Mid) hasn't grown too big compared to your safe funds. Reduce it slightly if needed to match your risk profile.
At the end of Year 5: Review if you've achieved your ₹8 Cr/month goal. If not, consider putting a bit more into the growth fund before retiring.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check your portfolio. If your stock portion (20%) has grown significantly due to a good market year and now looks too high for your comfort, move some money from stocks to the safer funds. Conversely, if the stock market dropped hard, you might consider adding more money to stocks instead of the safer parts to get back to your original 50/30/20 mix.
plan.rationale: You are 58 and want to retire comfortably but keep your wealth safe. Your goals are to create a fund for your children and to enjoy a life of 8 Crores/month. To do this, we split your money between safe options (50% overnight, 30% credit risk) and a bit of growth (20% large and mid stocks). This mix protects you from big drops while still growing your wealth steadily so you can reach your goals.
risks: Market volatility: Even though most of your money is in safe funds, the 20% in stocks can drop 10-20% in a bad year. Your overall portfolio will feel a bit bumpy.
Inflation risk: Fixed income and even some debt funds can't keep up with high inflation in the long run. Your money might not buy as much in the future as today.

Interest rate risk: If interest rates rise, the value of your debt funds might temporarily fall. Keep in mind, you'll get a good return over the long term if you stay invested for more than 3-4 years.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase your SIP by 5% every year to match inflation and rising living costs.

aps.reasoning: The plan allocates 100% of its equity component (20% of total) to a Large & Mid Cap mutual fund, and 0% to actively managed equity funds like sectoral or small-cap funds that have distinct active mandates. Therefore, the passive equity fraction is 1.0. The turnover score is high (0.9) because the plan explicitly recommends annual rebalancing (once a year) to maintain the 50/30/20 mix. While the equity portion has no individual stock picking mentions, the debt portion (80%) includes an actively managed 'Credit Risk' fund (a flexi-cap category in the scheme list), and the Overall Portfolio Activeness is low (0.3) because the strategy relies heavily on fixed-income stability and broad index-like equity exposure, making it largely passive in nature despite the active debt component.

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment is poor (0.3) because the investor's explicit goals require massive inflation-beating growth (₹8 Cr/month implies a corpus of ~70 Cr+ at age ~80, or substantial growth for inheritance), yet the plan caps equity at only 20% despite a 5-year horizon. A conservative investor 58 years old seeking such a high income multiple might fear volatility, but strictly speaking, a 20% equity allocation is mathematically insufficient to reach an 8 Crore/month retirement target from a 70 Lakh corpus in under a decade without significant external contributions or unrealistic assumptions. Diversification (0.8) is strong across asset classes and geographies. Risk-return appropriateness (0.2) is very low; the plan offers a 6.8% base return which is dangerously close to or below inflation for someone needing to multiply their wealth significantly over a 12-year retirement period, likely resulting in an inability to meet the ₹8 Cr goal. Internal consistency (0.9) is high; the rationale, allocations, and checkpoints tell a coherent, albeit unrealistic, story. Tax efficiency (0.95) is excellent; the plan correctly identifies the investor is in the 'new_regime' (no 80C), appropriately avoids offering ELSS, and selects fundamentally sound debt funds (Overnight, Credit Risk) and a Large & Mid cap fund which will benefit from LTCG structures without requiring aggressive slab optimization shortcuts.
